title: US-Iran Deal in Doubt as Rubio Rules Out Sanctions Relief for Hormuz Reopening
subhead: Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Congress the US will not trade sanctions relief for access to the Strait of Hormuz, signaling a harder line as Iran's Fars News reports diplomatic communications have halted. Meanwhile, Russia struck Kyiv with a massive drone and missile barrage after a week of threats, and Ukraine's battlefield momentum continues to build.

sections.heading: US Rejects Sanctions-for-Hormuz Framework
sections.body:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio told the Senate Foreign Relations Committee on Tuesday that the United States is not offering Iran sanctions relief in exchange for reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Testifying in his first congressional appearance since the Iran war began on February 28, Rubio said that any sanctions relief would be tied exclusively to Tehran giving up its nuclear program. The comments, reported by Reuters and Al-Monitor, represent a significant hardening of the US negotiating position.

Rubio defended the US military campaign as having degraded Iran's conventional military capabilities, noting that Tehran 'no longer has the conventional shield to hide behind.' He said an interim deal could 'happen today; it could happen tomorrow; it could happen next week,' but acknowledged there is no guarantee negotiations will produce an agreement acceptable to the Senate or the American public. 'The first thing that is a predicate to anything else happening—the straits have to be reopened,' Rubio told lawmakers.

The secretary also confirmed that Iran's new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, is 'increasingly engaging at some level' through intermediaries, despite not being seen publicly. Mojtaba succeeded his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the first day of US-Israeli strikes on February 28. Rubio's characterization contradicted earlier reports that the younger Khamenei was unconscious and receiving treatment in Qom.
sections.whyItMatters: The explicit rejection of sanctions-for-Hormuz narrows the diplomatic space for a near-term agreement. Iran has charged ships passage fees through the strait and demanded an end to Israeli operations in Lebanon as preconditions. By ruling out sanctions relief for Hormuz access, the US is betting that military pressure has weakened Tehran enough to extract concessions on nuclear issues.

sections.heading: Iran Suspends Message Exchange with US
sections.body:
Iran's semi-official Fars News agency reported Tuesday that the exchange of messages between Tehran and Washington via mediators stopped several days ago, contradicting President Donald Trump's assertion on Monday that talks with Iran were progressing 'at a rapid pace.' Fars quoted an unnamed 'informed source' saying Iran's last message to Washington conveyed a clear stance regarding its demand for an end to Israel's war with Hezbollah in Lebanon as part of any US-Iran agreement.

The same day, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it targeted the MSC Sariska V cargo ship in retaliation for a US strike on an Iranian vessel in the Sea of Oman. The IRGC claimed the vessel was American-Israeli owned; the shipping company MSC denied this, stating the ship was Italian-owned and that all crew members were safe. The IRGC also confirmed that 24 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz in the past 24 hours after obtaining authorization, and said more than 300 non-Iranian vessels have applied for safe passage since early May.

A senior IRGC commander, Brigadier General Mohammad Jafar Asadi, said Iran has not yet 'revealed all our trump cards' and warned that 'when surrender is not an option, war lies ahead.' The statements suggest Iran is preparing for a prolonged standoff rather than near-term accommodation.
sections.whyItMatters: The suspension of diplomatic messaging and continued IRGC maritime operations indicate that Tehran sees leverage in maintaining pressure rather than rushing to close a deal. The IRGC's control over Hormuz transit and willingness to target commercial shipping reinforces that leverage, even as the US maintains a naval blockade.

sections.heading: Russia Strikes Kyiv After Week of Threats
sections.body:
Russia launched hundreds of drones and dozens of missiles at Kyiv early Tuesday, killing at least 16 people according to Ukrainian authorities. The attack came more than a week after Moscow warned of a strike so large that diplomats and foreigners should flee the capital. The New York Times reported that Russian aircraft had spent days mimicking large attack formations, setting off alarms and wearing down residents before the actual strike arrived.

The bombardment matched the scale of two other deadly attacks on Kyiv last month, but analysts noted a psychological dimension: Russia's foreign ministry had issued dire warnings about retaliation for a Ukrainian drone strike that killed 21 students in the Russian-controlled Luhansk region. The delay between threats and strikes appeared designed to inflict a psychological toll, with Western envoys publicly vowing to stay in Kyiv to avoid signaling intimidation.

The attacks come at a difficult moment for Russia's war effort. According to a New York Times analysis, Moscow's advance in Ukraine has slowed nearly to a halt, with coerced mobilization in occupied territories and domestic recruitment falling short. Russia is stepping up coerced recruitment in occupied eastern Ukraine as its domestic efforts falter, and domestic discontent is reportedly growing.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's strikes on Kyiv demonstrate Moscow's continued ability to inflict pain on Ukrainian civilians, but they also reflect a war effort under strain. The combination of maximal threats and delayed strikes suggests the Kremlin is trying to compensate for battlefield losses with psychological warfare, while domestic pressures mount.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Momentum Builds as Russia's Falters
sections.body:
Ukraine's military position has strengthened significantly in recent months, according to multiple analysts. Franz-Stefan Gady, a Vienna-based military analyst, told The New York Times that 'Ukraine's position is much, much more formidable now than just a year ago.' Jack Watling of the Royal United Services Institute wrote in Foreign Affairs that 'in Kyiv, there is a growing optimism that Ukraine can fight Russia to a cease-fire,' noting that 'Russian combat performance is waning' despite constant drone strikes and shelling.

A detailed analysis in The i Paper identified a pivotal moment: Elon Musk's decision in February to block Russian access to Starlink satellites. The move disrupted communications and drone coordination across large sections of the front, with Ukrainian officials reporting a sharp drop in Russian bombardments on frontline positions. In the first five days after the Starlink cutoff, Ukrainian forces reportedly liberated more than 200 square kilometers—roughly equivalent to Russia's gains for all of December, according to the Institute for the Study of War.

Russia's casualty rate has reportedly escalated to over 1,000 soldiers per day in May, a level analysts described as unsustainable. Meanwhile, the European Union ratified a €90 billion loan for Ukraine, Britain agreed to supply 120,000 drones, and Ukraine continues its campaign of strikes on Russian energy infrastructure, forcing fuel rationing in the Moscow region.
sections.whyItMatters: The convergence of Ukrainian tactical innovation, Western financial support, and Russian logistical struggles has created the most favorable position for Kyiv in over a year. The Starlink decision illustrates how third-party technology policy can reshape battlefield dynamics, while the EU loan and British drone deal provide Kyiv with resources to sustain operations.

sections.heading: Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire Collapses Despite Trump Claim
sections.body:
Fighting between Israel and Hezbollah has continued despite President Trump's statement on Monday that he had persuaded both sides to halt attacks. CBS News reported that Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon killed eight people Tuesday, including a father and his two children. The Israeli military issued evacuation warnings for the southern Lebanese city of Nabatieh hours before peace talks between Israeli and Lebanese officials resumed in Washington.

Hezbollah has continued to strike northern Israel, with the group reporting 21 strikes against Israeli forces over a 24-hour period. Israeli public broadcaster Kan estimated that Hezbollah's drone capabilities are now limiting 80% of Israeli troop assaults in southern Lebanon. Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun said Tuesday that 'there is no option other than negotiation' to end the war, calling it 'the duty of the state to take care of its citizens.'

The disconnect between diplomatic claims and ground reality highlights the difficulty of separating the Israel-Hezbollah conflict from the broader US-Iran negotiations. Iran has demanded an end to Israeli operations in Lebanon as a precondition for any agreement with Washington, while Israel has continued to expand its campaign despite the ceasefire framework.
sections.whyItMatters: The Israel-Hezbollah fighting directly complicates US-Iran diplomacy, as Tehran frames Israeli operations as violations of the broader ceasefire. The continuation of strikes despite Trump's claim that both sides agreed to stop suggests that neither Israel nor Hezbollah is fully aligned with Washington's diplomatic timeline.

sections.heading: Baku Energy Week Highlights Southern Corridors to Europe
sections.body:
Global energy leaders gathered in Baku for Energy Week 2026 emphasized that southern energy corridors are set to dominate supplies to Europe as traditional routes face disruption. The focus comes as the Strait of Hormuz blockade continues to affect global oil and gas flows, with Iran charging fees for authorized transit.

Azerbaijan's Energy Minister Parviz Shahbazov held high-level meetings with EU officials to review strategic cooperation across key energy sectors. The discussions reflect Europe's accelerating effort to diversify energy sources away from Russian gas and Middle Eastern routes vulnerable to conflict. The first meeting of the Azerbaijan-US Economic Dialogue also took place during the forum.

The emphasis on southern corridors—from the Caspian through Turkey to Europe—illustrates how regional conflicts are reshaping global energy infrastructure. With Hormuz constrained and Russian gas flows diminished, alternative routes through the South Caucasus and Eastern Mediterranean have gained strategic importance.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's energy diversification drive creates new geopolitical alignments linking the Caspian region to European markets. The Baku forum demonstrates how energy infrastructure decisions made during the current conflicts will shape trade relationships and security dependencies for years to come.

bottomLine: The US-Iran negotiations are at a breaking point. Rubio's categorical rejection of sanctions relief for Hormuz access signals that Washington believes military pressure has given it leverage to demand nuclear concessions rather than a simple trade. Iran's suspension of diplomatic messaging and continued IRGC operations suggest Tehran calculates it can wait out American patience and exploit Israeli actions in Lebanon as a pretext. The simultaneous collapse of the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire underscores that these conflicts cannot be separated. Meanwhile, Ukraine's position has markedly improved—Russian Starlink losses, unsustainable casualties, and Ukrainian innovation have shifted momentum. The EU loan and British drone deal provide Kyiv resources, while Moscow's strikes on Kyiv demonstrate its continued ability to inflict civilian pain but also reveal a war effort under strain.
